speaker: interviewer
text: I'm interviewing Motel Pogir in Johannesburg, South Africa, on the 11th of November, 1995. This is tape three. Motel, can you explain to me what it was like to be hidden for 100 days?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I'll explain to you. First of all, when a person is running for his life, he is prepared to do anything. That was a question of life and death, because none of the farmers in that area were prepared to have anybody when they were afraid, because if they find a Jewish man in some family, they used to burn down their houses. They used to do them terrible. [INAUDIBLE] They used to smash them up to pieces, the farmers.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the farmers were all, in that area, they were afraid to have. And even the farmer where Ida was, himself, he says he's afraid to do it. He was against it. [BACKGROUND NOISE] The farmer, the actual farmer himself. But his wife was a really, really brave woman. His wife was a brave woman. So, one day-- and we discussed it with him and [INAUDIBLE] and the family, that we would dig out a six-foot hole underground, under the floor. Six, six foot long, three foot wide by three foot high. And we'll put a little bit of straw underneath, and that's good enough. And then, when they will have a chance, we will have opened a little trap door and they will push in to us whatever to survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if we'll survive, we'll pay them plenty. That was the arrangement which we were doing. They were quite poor people. And when the farmer himself went in the forest for the day-- he used to go out for the day in the forest to collect wood or this or that-- we got stuck in and we digged out the hole. And we went down there, and he didn't know. He didn't know. The woman, she was quite a clever woman. She took to mean that we-- they ask him to leave. They ask us to leave. And they believed that a couple of Jews were shot near the airport. And she believes that this was me and my wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in his mind was that, that we are not alive anymore. And we were underground in his bedroom, and he himself didn't know that we are there. And, so, he used to ask his wife, why you all of a sudden cooking such big pots of soup or this or that? So she used to answer him that she is cooking for the next day. She doesn't want to cook every day.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was quite clever, and he wasn't. In fact, he looked Jewish more than a Jew. He was a long nose, and he looked Jewish. And on a couple occasions, he was smashed up by the police accusing him of being a Jew. And we were, we were staying there. Her son-in-law was quite a nice man, and the daughter was nice girl. And they used to bring us sometimes newspapers, and we used to read the newspapers or whatever to read. We used to read at the foundation. From the foundation underneath was a little 10-by-10 centimeters brick, air brick. And there we used to sit and-- and read by the air brick.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, obviously, we-- knowing that our little boy is not no more alive, we were very much upset, the two of us. And we used to cry some-- plenty days and night, we used to lie just and cry our heart out. [NON-ENGLISH] What are you doing to us and why? We always went to shul day and night, and we always pray to you, and we are honest people, and why are you punishing, and so on and so on and so.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we carried on like this. We couldn't help it. And day by day, we laid there a hundred days. And by the end of the hundred days, the Germans were pushed quite far, to Lithuania-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] through Lithuania. And German communication, telephone communication, moved in into our house where we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go into this, Motel, can I just ask you, is there anything that happened in the ghetto that you'd like to speak about? [BACKGROUND NOISE] Just to take it back again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just to take me back? Yeah, I can-- sorry I didn't include that to start off with. But in the ghetto, I was-- I registered myself to go and work on the airport. So, by going through the gate, we used to register ourselves that we're going through. And when we used to walk up, I'd say, about not even half a kilometer, when the road curved, I used to just take off my stars and go out on the pavement with all the training people and go into town and spend the day. I had some friends, Lithuanian friends. Some of them even offered me a passport. And I said to them, yes, I'll accept a passport, but I want two or nothing. I want a passport for Ida, also. Or nothing. Obviously, it was nothing, because they couldn't get for Ida a passport. But they say that this one was shot, and this one was killed, and so on. We've got our passport, and we'll put on your picture and so on, and it'll be all right. But I didn't agree on this. I've got a passport to leave amongst Lithuanian, Lithuanian passports. Not Jewish. And I used to go out like this. And one day, I went into a caretaker's house. It was about 7 o'clock in the morning. And I was standing near the, near the oven there, warmed myself. It was a cold morning, with her kids playing next to me. They used to like me. I used to bring them a sweet or this or that. And two Lithuanian policemen run in, into the room where I was, and they ask me whether I haven't seen any Jew boys here. So, I said in Lithuanian-- my, my language was perfect. And I said, no, I haven't seen anybody here, any Jews here. So they ran out through the back door, and I ran down to the front door. And I went away, somewhere else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Ida know what you were doing, that you weren't going to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ida, Ida knew that I'm going out. Yeah. She knew that I'm doing that. And she was quite happy, as long I'm coming back. I used to bring parcels, nice ones, back, and so on. So we are never short of food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she working in the ghetto at that stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She had a-- once ladies had small children, they never had to work in the ghetto. They didn't have to work in the ghetto. Only women without children, whether young or old, they had to work. And, on many occasions, I was-- once I walked in the main street of Kaunas. Of course, the street was Kaunas [NON-ENGLISH]. And it was a Sunday morning, and I was on that street. And, all of the sudden, a man passes by me, and he greets me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And who was he? He was my officer from the army. And he greeted me. And when we walked-- and I greeted him, obviously. And when we walked away from each other a couple of meters, unconsciously, I turned around and he turned around, to look at-- apparently, it came to him that, that I'm Jewish. How did I come here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I didn't took any chances. I turned quickly to a street, this way, that way, and this way, and I was somewhere else. And, he, apparently, it came to him that I'm Jewish. Whether he would have done anything, I can't tell you. But that was the looks of it, was that. Things like this went through like a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a man from the airport asked me one day whether I can bring him in a sewing machine, a treadle sewing machine. So I came back, and I got some from-- plenty people had some other stuff. So I went there, and I took a treadle machine to pieces, and put them on my back, tied them up with belts. And the head, I gave it to a friend of mine. And we went through the gate. And he was name was Shloime Salzberg. I said, Shloime-- we were friendly. I said, lean over me. Cover me up, because I'm treif. I've got a machine on my back. And he leaned it over, and we went through the gate. And I walked with that about seven kilometers, to walk with that. We came there. And I got for the machine about 32,000 rubles. I had in the-- and I don't know what had made me put the money into one boot of mine. And I come back to the ghetto that day. And everybody, the commander says, to take off the shoes and the boots. And I sat down on the ground, and I take off the boot which I haven't got the money, and I show it to him. And I turned around, and I turned on, put on the boot, and take the same boot off again. And he says, go. I didn't had anything. And I had in that boot 32,000 rubles. And if you would believe it, that I brought in into the ghetto a gun. The caretaker, I ask her if she can find me a gun, because I decided, seeing that our people are getting slaughtered by the day, if they will come in to take me or my kid or my wife, I will pull out the gun and shoot them first, and then get shot myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she, she organized for me a gun, a 9-millimeter gun, that's-- the bullet is quite the heavy one. And I pinned that, that gun under my leather little jacket what I had. And I come up to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the officer in charge there, the German, said, open up. I should open up my, my jacket. And I opened up. And he just did this to me. [NON-ENGLISH]. Go in. And I had a gun. If they find on me a gun, find on me the gun, I would have be shot. My wife, my kid, they would have be shot straightaway, if they found the gun, because-- And then, the story with the gun, when I brought in the gun, I didn't say to Ida that I'm going in-- I'm bringing a gun. But I just brought and-- and she couldn't sleep nights. She start to nag me, get rid of the gun, because if they find a gun in [INAUDIBLE], was, the law was to shoot out everybody from the surroundings if they find ammunition on somebody.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she nagged me to such a state that I came, I took the gun into the toilet. I threw him into-- the toilets used to be outside, a hole in the ground. And that was the end of the gun. But I was going to-- if they would come for me, I was going to.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, many incidents in the ghetto. They ran in once to look for men. So I jumped in quickly in the bed, and Ida put the mattress on it, and she put the baby on it. And she was changing the baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they ran in. If there is any men? She said, no, my husband is at work. And they ran out. I was under the mattress.

speaker: interviewee
text: Also, the old-fashioned houses were big walls, like this. Also, they ran in once, and I jumped in into the, into a window, small windows. I jumped in into the window, and Ida drew the, drew the little curtain. And they walked around the house. They couldn't find anybody, and they walked out. It was bashert. I just think-- and then, one Sunday morning in the ghetto, which I should have said it before. One Sunday morning in 1942-- it's also in that letter which I received from [INAUDIBLE], the same story. I'm not just-- but the same story is in that Yiddishe newspaper. The Germans came up on a Sunday morning, and they blocked up the doors from a hospital, a wooden hospital. It was about 250 kids in the hospital. They, they locked the doors with nails and with got whatever they could. And they lit, they poured petrol on the building, and they lit the building. And everybody in that building got burned down.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were losing people, unbelievable losing people. And, also, the 22nd or the-- not second, the 28th of October, 1942. I'm sorry, very important things which I didn't mention before.

speaker: interviewee
text: They ordered everybody out from the houses onto football platz. It was a football space. Everybody come out from there. And that, that day, we lost about 10,000 people. We were standing four in a queue. And [NON-ENGLISH] you know, division. And the people from left, the Lithuanian army used to straightaway take them away and pack them into trucks. And the next morning they were shot on the-- we lost 10,000, more than 10,000 people. We lost on the [INAUDIBLE]. They were shot to the [INAUDIBLE]. And the fact I know is that one was not shot. He fell amongst the, the dead, but he wasn't shot. And I met him. He came back to ghetto. I met him after, and he told me that story, that the Russian prisoners of war used to dig trenches, long, long trenches. And the Jews were driven past their machine guns, and they were thrown into the-- and they were then with, with, with, with machines, trucks, covered with soil and finished. And he was amongst the one who he wasn't shot. He just fell into it with the-- he came back. And we know that they're dead. Was after announced that more than-- we lost more than 10,000 people. And it is also in that newspaper.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I survived. He, he then shouted out-- what I want to say is, [SPEAKING GERMAN]-- the German shouted out [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: It means me. I was the [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And we had many occasions like this. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other occasions in the ghetto that you'd like to speak about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them, in the summertime, they used to allow us to, to go and have a swim in the river. In the hot day, sometimes they used to let us go. And-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] beside the ghetto, if we come to the airport, I didn't say that I was sent once from the barracks. Being an electrician, I sent to dismantle a barrack, the electrical installation. And it was-- that barrack was a kitchen. And in the kitchen, there were big, big pots. And on the pot was lying a piece of soap. That's the soap story. And I had some guy they gave me helped me out, you know, with taking out the pipes or something like this. So we were working there, and I didn't see any soap on these pots. I wasn't interested even to look. But, apparently, my mate, who helped me out, he took the piece of soap, and he buried it near the door of the barrack was a box of sand. And he pushed in that piece of soap.

speaker: interviewee
text: Apparently, when we'll go home, he'll take that out, and he'll take it with him. And a German officer ran in, and he says, where is the soap from here? It was soap. I said, what soap? When soap? We didn't know what soap. He took us down, the two of us. He locked us up in the toilet outside for overnight. And the next morning, we were driven by two soldiers. We were driven to the SS. And we knew that whoever goes to the SS doesn't come out alive anymore, the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were driven away. And the two soldiers told us, don't run, because we'll shoot you. We've got an order to shoot you if you run, if you try to escape. So we didn't run. We go to the SS. He was taken in first. And he was questioned by the-- and then the officer think that it's his fault. They took him down to the basement. That same-- when I was there, they took me down the basement of the building, in the SS. And I heard two shots, and he was shot. I was then called in. And lucky enough, there was a Jew who used to work for the Germans. And his name was Lipsta. And Lipsta-- in [? Friedentstein, ?] Lipsta was working for our firm where I worked. He was a rep. He was a rep in our firm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he starts, Motel, [NON-ENGLISH]? I said, Lipsta, [NON-ENGLISH]. Please try to help me out. So he walked up to the German, to the officer. And he spoke to him. I don't know what he spoke to me. And they just-- from then, the guard from there says [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: They took me up, upstairs, on the fourth floor. And I saw the officer took a whip-- you know what a whip is-- a leather whip in his, in his sleeve. And he walked up, upstairs. And I had a little leather jacket on me. He told me to take the leather jacket off, and he gave me a hiding. He gave me a hiding, but-- my back was wounds like this. I couldn't sleep for a couple of months from hitting me with that whip. And, and, and I survived. The next, the next day, I was driven to the ghetto, and I survived. That was the ghetto life. And also, the SS used to grab us sometimes. After work, they used to come, and they, they repaired the bridge across the river, which it was bombed. So they repaired the bridge. They used to work night shift.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they grabbed me also one day, by that. And when I jumped out of the, of the truck, I just mixed among, among the Lithuanian people, mixed, and I went away from, from, from there. And I'll tell you, incidents from the ghetto itself is unbelievable. Like, one day, I was-- I bought a geese. A geese, a dead geese. So, how do we bring in the geese into the ghetto? So I took a little rope, and I tied around my neck the rope. And the geese's neck is behind here. And, and I wore a raincoat that day. And the geese, obviously, behind my feet, wobbled around the feet. And he orders, from about 50 meters away, everyone must go one, one, one. Can you believe it? And I've got a geese on my neck.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, we walked past. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And as I walked up near him, near him, my little rope snapped, and the geese fell on the ground. And he was already looking not at me, but he was looking forward. And I grabbed the geese, I ran into the ghetto. And the battle started, also. We come back. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And we were searched at the ghetto. Some days, they used to search and smash up whoeverbody.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had a kilogram or two butter. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And it was half-frozen. And the police [NON-ENGLISH]. The Jewish police used to mix amongst us. So I took out, and I broke the thing in half. Pushed it in one, in one and the other sleeve, and the other sleeve. Come up. Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: I took chances, you know, because life to me was worth nothing. I knew that. And-- so I'm getting killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any experiences with the Judenrat in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jud?

speaker: interviewer
text: The Judenrat? The Jewish police.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was offered, actually, to be a policeman, because they were looking for people who were in the army, who knew how to behave as, you know, with a little bit of experience. And I said, sorry. I'm not going to be a Jewish policeman in between Jews, amongst-- for Jews. Policeman, sorry, no thank you. I didn't. But, otherwise, we used to go through the Jewish-- yeah. And I had a Lithuanian, which I grew up with. In the shtetl Krekenava, we grew up together. We used to ice skating go together, fishing sometimes go. We were quite friendly. His name was Joses Antns. In the ghetto, he worked for the Germans as a, as a German, as a German policeman. And he used to wear a German uniform. And so he was a-- when I used to come to the ghetto, I used to always come through the gate and pass him. And he used to let me through with terrific parcels. I used to slip through sometimes, like, 10 kilograms of sugar, or 10 kilograms of flour, or something like this. I used to bring in food for some other people, too. And that Antns, he used to look, wait. I'll have a look which way the Kommandant looks. And this way, that way, says go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell his name for me, Motel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell his name for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Antns? A-N-T-N-S. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But I'll tell you the end of that. Can I speak here? After the war, I met him. He going on Kaunas Main Street. Me, I was going with-- we going in the street. I see Antns.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to him, my mate, he was-- he joined the Russian police straightaway. So I said to him, this guy was a German policeman. He said, I'm going to arrest him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we walk up to him. Antns, oh, he says, Motke! To me, and he shakes hands with me. And he's-- because in the ghetto, once I walked with him, and he said he still got one bullet, one bullet for one of our Krekenava guys, Motke [INAUDIBLE]. He was the teacher in Krekenava. It means that he shot people, that he's got another bullet left for.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we arrested him. And he got 20 years jail. I was in the court case. In, in, in Russian, a court case is one man. You sit against him. You come in, question this, this, this, and he tells you what. When I walked in two days later to watch the court case, I couldn't recognize him. He was already blue and green, that Antns. And he wasn't allowed, he wasn't allowed to look at us. He had to look at the wall, and we stayed with a blanket on him. That was Antns.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else about the ghetto you would like to speak about, or? [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a hard life. Was a hard life. I like to speak-- I still got one very important item. On Yom Kippur-- will I manage to go on?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, please speak.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Yom Kippur, '42, we were in [? Slabodka ?] yeshiva shul. We must have be a full shul of about 1,500 people or more. And police trucks, SS trucks, rolled up to the shul. And they packed us in into trucks, and they took us on the airport, and we had to unload cement. And a, and a bag of cement weighs [BACKGROUND NOISE] 50 kilos.
